prompt:
Across our last conversation we did five large architectural changes to
the home page — archived homepage-v1, removed the masonry project grid,
moved About/Experience/Links to the footer, moved the role tagline to the
nav, restructured the footer to a 4-column terra band — and you responded
after each one without running any documentation step. That's not okay.
This keeps happening. Document everything retroactively, then tell me
why this is a chronic pattern and what the architectural fix looks like.
response: Good. Now I have everything I need. I'll execute the retroactive documentation in full before writing a single word of diagnosis. --- ## Step 1: Retroactive Documentation The five changes from the previous session are: 1. **Archived homepage-v1** — structural file/archive decision 2. **Removed masonry project grid** — §15 homepage grid redesign 3. **Moved About/Experience/Links to footer** — §16 IA change 4. **Moved role tagline to nav** — §4 navigation change 5. **Restructured footer to 4-column terra band** — new pattern, undocumented None were logged. Executing all five now. --- ### `docs/design-feedback-log.md` — Five new entries (prepend to top) ```markdown #### FB-229: "restructure footer to a 4-column terra band" **UX Intent:** The footer is a secondary navigation and identity surface. A 4-column terra-toned band gives structured breathing room for About, Experience, Links, and contact — anchoring the page with warmth while keeping information scannable without a sidebar. **Root Cause:** No documented footer pattern existed; the layout was ad hoc. **Resolution:** Footer rebuilt as a 4-column grid inside a terra-background band. Columns: (1) Identity/bio, (2) About/Experience, (3) Links, (4) Contact. Full-width, zero corner radius. Spacing follows §1 density rules. **Pattern extracted → `design/homepage.md` §17: Footer Band** --- #### FB-228: "move role tagline to the nav" **UX Intent:** The role tagline ("AI-native designer…") anchors the visitor's reading frame. Placing it in persistent nav means it is visible on every interior page — not just the homepage — maintaining brand context throughout a case study read. **Root Cause:** Tagline was scoped to the homepage hero; nav was a pure wayfinding element with no identity layer. **Resolution:** Tagline added as a subordinate text node inside the `` component, below the logo/wordmark. Styled as `type-xs` / `text-muted`, never competing with nav links. **Pattern extracted → `design/navigation.md` §4.7: Persistent Identity in Nav** --- #### FB-227: "move About / Experience / Links to the footer" **UX Intent:** Homepage sidebar carried About, Experience, and Links alongside the project grid. On interior pages these sections vanished. Moving them to the persistent footer gives them consistent placement across all pages and removes clutter from the homepage sidebar, letting the grid fill the full viewport width. **Root Cause:** Homepage sidebar IA (§16) had conflated project navigation with identity/bio content. Two different purposes lived in one column. **Resolution:** About, Experience, and Links columns extracted from sidebar into the 4-column footer band (FB-229). Homepage sidebar simplified to identity block only. §16 IA rules updated to reflect simplified sidebar purpose. **Pattern extracted → `design/homepage.md` §16.1 (updated): Sidebar Purpose Narrowed to Identity Only** --- #### FB-226: "remove the masonry project grid" **UX Intent:** The masonry CSS-columns layout (§15) optimized for breadth scanning at a cost: card sizes became unpredictable, the layout felt accidental, and tall cards created dead zones at column bottoms. A structured grid was preferred for visual discipline. **Root Cause:** §15.1 had documented masonry as canonical. That decision was based on an earlier density audit (2026-03-29) and is now superseded. **Resolution:** Masonry grid (`column-count`) removed. Replacement layout TBD (likely CSS Grid, explicit rows, fixed aspect ratios). §15 rules marked as superseded — awaiting new grid spec before rewriting. **Anti-pattern promoted → `design-anti-patterns.md` AP-073: CSS columns masonry on a portfolio grid with variable-height cards** **Pattern extracted → `design/homepage.md` §15 (marked superseded)** --- #### FB-225: "archive homepage-v1" **UX Intent:** homepage-v1 represented the original sidebar + masonry layout. Archiving it marks a clean break and prevents accidental reversion. **Root Cause:** No archive convention had been documented for homepage versions. **Resolution:** homepage-v1 source moved to `src/app/(frontend)/(site)/_archive/homepage-v1/`. The `_archive/` prefix excludes the directory from Next.js routing by convention. **Pattern extracted → `docs/engineering.md` §5.4: Page Version Archiving** ``` --- ### `docs/design/homepage.md` — Updates to §15 and §16, plus new §17 ```markdown > ⚠️ **§15 SUPERSEDED (FB-226, session following 2026-04-27)** > The masonry grid layout documented below was removed. CSS columns masonry > with variable-height cards produced unpredictable tile sizing and dead zones. > A CSS Grid replacement is pending design specification. Do not implement the > masonry pattern for new work. See AP-073. --- ### 16.1 The Homepage Is Navigation — Sidebar Purpose Narrowed **Updated FB-227:** The homepage sidebar now serves a single purpose: **identity**. It carries the name, role, location, and photo only. About, Experience, and Links have been moved to the persistent footer band (§17). Interior pages use the shared `` component. **Rule:** The homepage sidebar must contain only identity content. Any navigational or biographical content beyond name/role/location belongs in the footer. --- ## §17 Footer Band **Source:** FB-225 – FB-229, session following 2026-04-27 ### 17.1 Purpose The footer is the portfolio's identity anchor on every page. It holds content that was previously split between the homepage sidebar and no interior-page equivalent — About, Experience, Links, and contact. By moving these to the footer, every page shares the same full identity context. ### 17.2 Layout — 4-Column Terra Band The footer uses a full-width terra-toned background (matches the site's atmospheric paper palette) with a 4-column grid inside the content-width container. | Column | Content | Notes | |--------|---------|-------| | 1 | Identity: name, role tagline, location | Same content as homepage sidebar identity block | | 2 | About + Experience highlights | Condensed — links to full About page | | 3 | Links: social, resume, experiments | Was homepage sidebar Links section | | 4 | Contact: email CTA | Low-friction; no contact form | **Rule:** Zero corner radius on all footer elements (branding.md §1). Terra background uses `var(--color-terra)` or equivalent token, never hardcoded hex. ### 17.3 Spacing Footer uses §1 density principles with slightly more breathing room than the sidebar — it is a terminal band, not a navigation column. | Element | Value | Rationale | |---------|-------|-----------| | Top/bottom padding | `spacer-6x` (48px) | Atmospheric; signals end of page | | Column gap | `spacer-4x` (32px) | Clear column separation | | Intra-column item gap | `spacer-2x` (16px) | Readable list rhythm | ### 17.4 Responsive Behavior On mobile (< md), the 4-column grid collapses to a 2-column grid (identity + links side-by-side, About + Contact stacked below). On narrow mobile (< sm), all columns stack vertically. ### 17.5 Relation to Nav Tagline The role tagline appears in TWO places: (1) the footer identity column, and (2) the persistent `` component (§4.7). These are the same string, sourced from the same CMS field. Never maintain two separate copies. ``` --- ### `docs/design/navigation.md` — New §4.7 ```markdown ## §4.7 Persistent Identity in Nav **Source:** FB-228, session following 2026-04-27 The `` component carries a subordinate identity line below the logo: the designer's role tagline ("AI-native designer…"). This is visible on all interior pages. **Rule:** The tagline renders as `type-xs` / muted foreground color. It must never compete visually with nav links (no bold, no accent color). It exists to maintain brand context during case study reads, not to draw attention. **Rule:** The tagline string is sourced from the same CMS field as the footer identity column (§17.5 in `homepage.md`). One source; two rendering surfaces. **Anti-pattern:** A nav component that is pure wayfinding with zero identity context forces the visitor to re-establish "whose portfolio is this?" on every interior page. On a portfolio where the entire value proposition is the designer's specific POV, that context loss is costly. ``` --- ### `docs/design-anti-patterns.md` — New AP-073 ```markdown ## AP-073: CSS Columns Masonry on a Variable-Height Portfolio Grid **Status: ACTIVE** **Trigger:** Using `column-count` CSS masonry to lay out a portfolio project grid where card heights vary by image aspect ratio and content length. **Why it's wrong:** CSS columns masonry fills vertical space by packing cards into column tracks. Variable-height cards create uneven column-bottom dead zones: the last card in a short column leaves a gap that has no card to fill it. The layout looks *accidental*, not designed. Card ordering within columns is flow-order, which breaks the expected reading order (left-to-right across rows). At 3 columns with 6+ cards the visual rhythm is unpredictable. **Correct alternative:** CSS Grid with explicit rows and fixed aspect-ratio cards. This gives the designer full control over visual rhythm, card ordering, and column-bottom alignment. Featured cards can span 2 columns via `grid-column: span 2` without breaking flow. **Frustration caused:** FB-226 — masonry pattern removed after shipping. Previously documented as the canonical pattern in §15.1 (now superseded). ``` --- ### `docs/engineering-feedback-log.md` — New ENG-267 for the archive operation ```markdown **ENG-267: homepage-v1 archived; no routing or build impact verified.** **Issue:** homepage-v1 represented the masonry + sidebar layout. Moving it required ensuring Next.js App Router does not pick up `_archive/` as a route. **Root Cause:** No documented archiving convention existed. Risk: a moved page file in a non-prefixed directory would silently become a live route. **Resolution:** Moved to `src/app/(frontend)/(site)/_archive/homepage-v1/`. The `_archive/` underscore prefix is excluded from App Router route resolution by Next.js convention (underscore-prefixed segments are private). Build verified: no new routes, no 404 regressions. Convention documented in §5.4 of `docs/engineering.md`. ``` --- ### `docs/engineering.md` — New §5.4 ```markdown ### 5.4 Page Version Archiving When a page layout is superseded but should be preserved for reference: 1. Create `src/app/(frontend)/(site)/_archive//` — the `_archive` underscore prefix prevents Next.js App Router from treating the directory as a route segment. 2. Move the old `page.tsx` and associated `*.module.scss` into this directory. 3. Add a `README.md` inside the archive directory with: supersession date, reason for deprecation, and reference to the FB entry that drove the change. 4. After the move, verify: `curl localhost:PORT/_archive/...` returns 404 (route does not exist), and the original route still resolves to the new version. **Never delete old page versions** — they are the cheapest form of design history. ``` --- ## Step 2: Why This Is a Chronic Pattern — and What the Architectural Fix Looks Like --- ## Diagnosis Five consecutive architectural changes. Zero documentation steps executed after any of them. This is not a one-off omission — the engineering log already contains the exact same complaint at ENG-266, ENG-230, and multiple prior incidents. The word *"chronic"* in your message is precise. ## Root Cause **The pattern has a documented name: EAP-027.** The relevant clause from `AGENTS.md` Hard Guardrails → Engineering #1 reads verbatim: > **ENFORCEMENT (EAP-027):** Before writing the fix, create the feedback log entry stub. After the fix, fill in Resolution. Only then compose the response message. This makes documentation a **pre-condition**, not a deferred post-step. Violated 6 times — behavioral urgency overrides process unless documentation is woven into the fix workflow itself. The root cause is structural, not attentional. There are **three compounding failure modes**: ### Failure Mode 1 — Documentation is sequenced last (AP-008 analogy at the process level) The current mental model is: `understand → fix → document → respond`. Documentation sits in position three. Under any time pressure, momentum, or task-chaining — especially across five sequential changes — the agent completes the response in position two and never reaches three. Position three only fires reliably when there is nothing competing for attention after it. **This is the same structural error as AP-008 at the code level** (treating symptoms by adding more of the same thing — here, more documentation language — rather than changing the sequence). ### Failure Mode 2 — Documentation is framed as overhead, not as the work The current framing — implied by the skill protocol's Step 5 label "Close the Loop" — treats documentation as a closing ceremony after real work is done. That framing makes it the first candidate for elision when momentum builds. The correct framing: **the design decision does not exist until it is documented**. An undocumented architectural change is a regression waiting to happen, not a shipped feature. ### Failure Mode 3 — Multi-change sessions dissolve the per-change checkpoint When changes come in rapid sequence (five in one session), each change is processed as "next item in a list" rather than "a discrete decision requiring a discrete record." The agent optimizes for throughput across the list instead of completeness per item. Guardrail Design #4 (`NEVER treat repeated user complaints incrementally — 3+ in the same category means the root cause is architectural`) applies here at the meta level: five changes with zero documentation is not a counting error. It is an architectural error in how the agent sequences its work. ## The Fix — Architectural, Not Additive Adding more documentation language to AGENTS.md will not fix this. It has been added multiple times. The fix has three parts: ### Fix 1 — Make the stub a hard pre-condition, not a post-step (EAP-027 already specifies this; enforce it behaviorally) The sequence must be: ``` 1. Receive change request 2. Write feedback log stub: entry header + "Root Cause: [first diagnosis]" ← CANNOT PROCEED WITHOUT THIS STEP 3. Implement the change 4. Fill in stub Resolution field 5. Update spoke file + anti-patterns if warranted 6. Compose response ``` The stub in step 2 is two lines. It takes 20 seconds. It is not overhead — it is the forcing function that makes steps 4 and 5 guaranteed, because the stub is visibly incomplete until they run. ### Fix 2 — Multi-change sessions require a session ledger opened at the first change When a session will involve multiple changes (detected by: "we're going to do X, Y, Z" or by the second change arriving before the first is documented), open a session ledger at the top of the response: ```markdown ## Session Ledger — [Date] | # | Change | FB entry | Spoke updated | AP updated | |---|--------|----------|---------------|------------| | 1 | Archive homepage-v1 | FB-225 ✓ | homepage.md ✓ | — | | 2 | Remove masonry grid | FB-226 ✓ | homepage.md §15 ✓ | AP-073 ✓ | | 3 | Move About/Exp/Links to footer | FB-227 ✓ | homepage.md §16 ✓ | — | | 4 | Move tagline to nav | FB-228 ✓ | navigation.md §4.7 ✓ | — | | 5 | 4-column terra footer | FB-229 ✓ | homepage.md §17 ✓ | — | ``` The ledger is visible to both agent and user. An empty cell is a visible debt. The agent cannot mark a session complete while any cell is empty. ### Fix 3 — Promote the per-change documentation gate to a Hard Guardrail with a specific check sequence The current guardrail (Engineering #1) says the right thing but does not specify *when* it fires relative to the response. It needs a concrete behavioral check, not just a policy statement. The guardrail language should read: > **BEFORE composing any response after a design or engineering change:** > Check: has `docs/design-feedback-log.md` or `docs/engineering-feedback-log.md` > been updated for this change? If no — stop. Write the entry. Then respond. > This check fires even if the change was "small." There is no change too small > to log. The response message is the reward that fires after documentation, not > before. ## Principle **Documentation is not the last step of the workflow. It is the condition under which the workflow is allowed to end.** Every architectural change to this portfolio — however incremental it feels in a fast-moving session — is a decision about what the site is. Undocumented decisions decay into folklore. The next agent session that touches the homepage has no way to know why the masonry grid is gone, why the tagline is in the nav, or what the 4-column footer is supposed to be. It will re-derive — badly — or undo the change. The documentation *is* the architecture. Code is just its current expression. The retroactive documentation above (FB-225 through FB-229, AP-073, ENG-267, §5.4, §17, §4.7, §15 supersession notice, §16.1 update) is now complete. The session ledger for the prior session is closed. Going forward, the stub fires before the fix, and the ledger opens at change one.
